name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: The volume of details. Took a while to understand flow
learnedToday: Adjust communications depending on who you are talking to. Tech/Business

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: The interactions with the bots. It was very realistic and human like.
learnedToday: I glossed over a question from shay to confirm the fix instead to ask for a root cause analysis. I would have completed the drill quicker if I had have prioritised the ask from shay. So i'll pay more attention to that going forward.

name: Roblox Workshop
surprises: 2 issues coinciding together was interesting
learnedToday: good to re confirm with the teams on the scope of the issues to identify that its not just 1

name: Roblox Workshop
surprises: Two incidents for the price of one! one not as severe as the other. engaging 3rd parties, remote hands
learnedToday: Prioritising the issues when faced with more than one, kind of related but not really directly

name: Roblox Workshop
surprises: Having 2 issues at once.
learnedToday: Be sure to understand which issue is a priority.

name: Roblox Workshop
surprises: Panick from uninformed stakeholders. Lack of complaints / any drop in traffic. Given ad services were down expected to see a bigger drop in order totals
learnedToday:
Good to stop. Summarise and then ask where issues could be stemming from.
Don't assume all issues are related

name: Roblox Workshop
surprises: multiple issues at once
learnedToday: need to clearly report to business comms if there are multiple issues at once

name: Roblox Workshop
surprises: There were two separate issues happening
learnedToday: To involve Infra team earlier as needed to reach out to third party

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
surprises: Senior members of team dismissive of junior
learnedToday: That its not always the junior team members change that causes issues

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
surprises: none
learnedToday: was quite realistic, dan trying to downplay the chance it was his change and then reluctantly rolling back

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
surprises: the engineer stating the change wouldnt have impacted anything when it did.
learnedToday: check what region was out.

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
surprises: Nothing unexpected - it was a helpful drill, high pressure.
learnedToday:
I think I should be more assertive with the engineers to get services restored rather than being passive.
I think I improved with the comms from the last drill and kept the business updated.

name: Rumours
surprises: it was simple to me as its one of the first things i check when i see an issue that no one else is experiencing.
learnedToday: DTA.

name: Rumours
surprises: Good to have an incident which isnt customer impacting but serious
learnedToday: Not over complicate

name: Rumours
surprises: none have seen many of these local isseus for high level employees
learnedToday: always check impact and start with local issues

name: Rumours
surprises: nothing really unexpected. good to see an isolated incident rather than specific to a change thats impacting an entire region/the whole customer set.
learnedToday: checking with the wider team around other reports, checking local settings/issues.

name: Rumours
surprises: Local issue was new.
learnedToday: Less severe issues should still be escalated. Need to remember this.

name: The Chain Workshop
surprises: Team members jumping in later than normal. Usually hands on sooner, but more realistic. Multi-change impact was a good touch
learnedToday:
Can be isolated to multiple changes
Should have put a banner on the website while troubleshooting

name: The Chain Workshop
surprises: That there were 3 changes needing rolled back. And issue wasnt immediatly fixed following rollbacl
learnedToday: To not give up :)

name: The Chain Workshop
surprises: Change fixed it temporarily not permanently
learnedToday: Wait before confirming with users

name: The Chain Workshop
surprises: timing on when changes were made differing to when issue occured
learnedToday:
The practice of restoration over root cause analysis helped to eliminate possible causes of the incident and led to faster restoration time.

Using grounding when communicating with the team to establish a common ground, in doing this the team became more organized as they had a mutual understanding of the current state of the drill.

name: The Chain Workshop
surprises: Having to react to what was initially perceived to be root cause not actually being the complete case - having to push further to identify root cause and resolve
learnedToday:
Effective communication between stakeholders and Bez
Not to assume the first 'obvious' smoking gun is the complete root cause

name: The Chain Workshop
surprises: Changes rolled back but could not see the issue resolved - perhaps a cache issue with the website or part of the drill that issues dont resolve instantly
learnedToday: Always verify the incident is fully resolved

name: The Chain Workshop
surprises: multiple changes having a knock on effect
learnedToday: communicating efficiently the issue to each party

name: Cart-astrophe
surprises: That changes were pushed by Shay with no crosscheck
learnedToday: Not sure

name: Cart-astrophe
surprises: The fact that Tanya has pushed two recent PROD changes, but was AWOL meant that we had limited information on her changes and validation steps. We needed to roll back all related changes in the past day to restore.
learnedToday: It's important to make sure that people prioritize the major incident instead of customer calls.

name: Cart-astrophe
surprises: Tanya not being available, this happens in the real world so a good test.
learnedToday: Cross check all changes within the same period, I checked and linked 2/3 but missed one. Additionally, important to escalate early in the event of an issue if a team member is not responding.

name: Cart-astrophe
surprises: Use grounding to communicate the current working theory. During the incident, it's important to ensure the incident response team has the same mutual understanding of the current state of play.
learnedToday: Dont relay on one person to fix a change

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: the third party element was very good
learnedToday: always assess the whole supply chain

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: interesting to do a drill that was an issue with the 3rd party vendor
learnedToday: escalating with vendors when the incident is outside of the organisations control, looping in the right people to escalate

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: More questions and expectations from Bez.
learnedToday: Be more aware of the business implications and relay these to Bez earlier.

name: Radio Silence
surprises: Having to chase up when people are being unresponsive
learnedToday: Reprioritising tasks appropriately for the rest of the team (Tanya being DND as on other deliverables)

name: Radio Silence
surprises: Engineers being off or not available.
learnedToday: Learn the website better.

name: Radio Silence
surprises: Couldn't get to the bottom of it
learnedToday: Still learning

name: Radio Silence
surprises:
Issue with the website that went unnoticed
Team members unavailable
learnedToday:
Verifying the issues with different metrics. Generic of "drop in sales" , what has dropped etc.
Checking each service that may have been effected in a change

name: Version confusion
surprises: The severity wasn't high to start but more users started reporting the issue as the drill went on
learnedToday: Take control even when team members are cooperating

name: Version confusion
surprises: Lower impact issue, engineering using wrong data
learnedToday: Fact check with the lead of that area

name: Version confusion
surprises: #NAME?
learnedToday: #NAME?

name: Version confusion
surprises: Team wasn't sure if the issues reported were significant enough to form an incident.
learnedToday: Staying calm & not jumping the gun while asking valid questions to quantify the problem is key in p1 scenarios.

name: Version confusion
surprises: I think the evolving change in the impact of users from 0.09% to 5-8% changed the priority of the incident, it went from being low impact issue that allowed us time to assess and review the impact, to suddenly being a bigger issue that became more urgent.
learnedToday: I think getting a clear view of all the recent changes, the details, and what it would take to resolve a bug on our platform is really important to understand.

name: The Switch
surprises: new member of the team
learnedToday: check that nothing has been compromised after issue resolved

name: The Switch
surprises: The world building in terms of network infrastructure
learnedToday: to utilise the full team

name: The Switch
surprises: A cyber security engineer wouldn't be included so early on in an event like this within my organisation, it would be heavily relied on by the networks team
learnedToday: To utilise the full team, and to use teamwork to help fill those knowledge gaps

name: The Switch
surprises: Network and Security vulnerabilities being the prime focus was new and surprising
learnedToday: Failing over is a good route to avoid restarts, make sure vulns are patched in timely manor

name: The Switch
surprises: Amount of time for some responses.
learnedToday:
Continue to chase bots for updates and ideas
Quantify the impact before updating the business. Get % differences etc.

name: The Switch
surprises:
The team worked together well. 
The network was only slow for certain members - not across the board
learnedToday: Make sure to check on the switch side if there are issues with the network

name: The Switch
surprises: The issue wasnt affecting all users
learnedToday: Involve the security team sooner as it was network related

name: The Switch
surprises: Security Analyst also acted as part of what I would normally consider the networks team .
learnedToday: Not everything is application related.

name: Details Matter
surprises: The issue was hard to recreate at first as the issue was specific to certain regions and currency, so important to try and scope out the true impact and not waste time at the start.
learnedToday: Keeping on top of all the different message streams coming through was difficult; need to pause and take time to think about it clearly.

name: Details Matter
surprises: Unclear how we could have got to the root cause before rolling back the change.
learnedToday: Okay to not fully understand the issue but to know the team / roles well in order to manage an incident effectively.

name: Breaking Bez
surprises: Decision making and high pressure from CEO. No clear 'right answer'
learnedToday: Prioritise restoration and stay calm

name: Breaking Bez
surprises: Unlike other drills, i wasn't able to decipher what was going to happen, AC fix or Failover
learnedToday: I might not have all the answers but I can get help from the team in diagnosing

name: Breaking Bez
surprises: That the final decision was up to just me to make
learnedToday: To be decisive and to call out individual team members to look at different things

name: Breaking Bez
surprises: Issue was not internal, therefore we had less control over how to fix it.
learnedToday: This incident required me to make an executive decision under pressure, with the information that I had. It was important for me to be clear in my intentions and back the choice 100%.

name: Breaking Bez
surprises: delegating the tasks to the team
learnedToday: how to perform under pressure and to go off your own judgement

name: Breaking Bez
surprises: Use grounding to communicate current working theory. During the incident, it's important to ensure the incident response team has the same mutual understanding of the current state of play.
learnedToday: DR service should always be clearly documented, makes it far easier to manage. Resiliency is there for a reason, it should be used.

name: Breaking Bez
surprises: Despite some engineers providing their own opinion, their thinking was based on unknown conditions, such as a DC fixing their cooling system which is out of our control. Being confident in your BCP testing and processes is important to ensure stability.
learnedToday: Always be confident in your company processes

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: The nature of the problem was an interesting one, needed to think outside the box
learnedToday: It taught me about the difference between a DOS and DDOS attack!

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: more grounding required, understandable with the more advanced drills. Guilty of assuming the bots have read the whole chat
learnedToday: Where are we seeing the errors? Is it isolated to one system, country, subset

name: Memory Lane Workshop
surprises: Calls for immediate reboots to a production live system.
learnedToday: Important to not react to quickly into rash decisions, people will call for drastic action but to remain calm, pull all information in and go from there.

name: Too Good To Be True Workshop
surprises: much much more relaistic than i had imagined
learnedToday: importance of busniess comms to seniors

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: No change control
learnedToday: n

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: the technical depth.
learnedToday: always question when someone says they just pushed it to UAT....

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: i found it difficult to get a response on flipping to DR and then it was going to take too long - i guess this is part of the game
learnedToday: always check live operations as well as past changes

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: The issue was related to a test being ran
learnedToday: Daniel said he ran the tests in UAT so it shouldnt affect it but that wasnt the case and it was the issue in the end

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: I enjoyed this drill! Nothing really surprising/unexpected.
learnedToday: Shows human errors (lack of change control and documentation of changes) - good to note in future that cant always trust the changes listed.

name: Memory Lane
surprises: Incident resolved then broke again
learnedToday: Don't confirm completionor proper resolution until 100% sure

name: Memory Lane
surprises: That business were asking for more innfo
learnedToday: not to always go first option to resolve

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Hamed & Tonus not being OOO
learnedToday: Logs never lie!!!

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: The pushback from bots
learnedToday: It was too similar to real life difficulties with getting the right people together in order to progress findings, reach resolution

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: The requirement for approval
learnedToday: Dont be afraid to escalate higher for approval when needed

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: The approval process surprised me, the fact that Bez could not sign off on a technical change, I feel as though if he was the only one available and with such high authority he would have the right to sign off on this.
learnedToday: It was good that people were out of office, the drill showed me how to utilise the team on hand.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: the out of office!
learnedToday: escalation and resource management

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Having Tinus and Hamed OOO made it quite stressful, and i didn't think i had the ability to approve changes on their behalf... Chaotic incident overall!
learnedToday: I think taking the time to really think hard about the steps to run through, it felt there was a lot of information coming in , and was difficult to get a clear understanding of the issue.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: the difficulty. This felt the most real world scenario yet.
learnedToday: we restored service but i didnt check to see or notice when that actually happened. i just presumed we were still down due to the conversations we were having.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Having to repeatedly asked Tanya to step away
learnedToday: Not to listen to others and use the facts / data to start investigation

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Not straightforward, required several restarts and high impact decisions.
learnedToday: Get everyone on board when a p1 incident is on

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: That took multiple steps to resolve
learnedToday: To make decisions fast

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Even though it was a certs issue, it was a more intricate fix that was not immediately obvious.
learnedToday: Dig deeper when changes are made.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: More decision making required
learnedToday: Deciding what option will be best overall in order to resolve incident

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: the escalation contacts being ooo and the need for a maintenance banner
learnedToday: sometimes temporary maintenance is essential for critical issues

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Unexpected to have characters out of office.
learnedToday: Need to work on getting characters to elaborate on what they're seeing. Even if something is "okay"- why is it okay? / Can we XC details

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Engineers being extremely uncooperative, made it challenging when no escalation was available
learnedToday:
don't get hung up on others trying to speculate (email maintenance)
maybe dont schedule involved maintenance when every other member of the team is unavailable.... :)
